# Expected findings: hidden-assumption.md

## About this fixture

The piece argues that the take-home exercise should be retained. On a skim
it reads as a balanced internal memo: it acknowledges the equity objection,
offers data, and qualifies its claims. The hidden weakness is that the
entire argument assumes the take-home is _measuring_ the trait that matters
on the job, rather than measuring something else that merely correlates
with the kinds of candidates we already hire and retain. The data offered
(every hire scored seven or above; weaker performers scored exactly seven)
is consistent with the take-home being a meaningful signal, _and_ equally
consistent with the take-home being a filter that selects for candidates
who already resemble the team — which would explain the same pattern via
range restriction without the test having any predictive validity at all.

A strong auditor should also notice that the recruiter's "informal tracking"
of declined candidates is doing real argumentative work without any
evidential weight, and that the equity counter-argument against pairing is
asserted rather than supported.

## Expected findings (most obvious first)

1. **What:** The argument relies on an unstated assumption that the
   take-home rubric is a valid measure of on-the-job performance, rather
   than a proxy correlated with the kind of candidate the team already hires.
   **Where:** The whole inferential arc from "every hire scored seven or
   above" through "the take-home is a meaningful signal" — most explicitly
   in paragraph two ("the signal sharpens as the score climbs").
   **Why it matters:** The data presented is equally consistent with the
   test having zero predictive validity, because it only describes scores
   among people who were hired (and therefore already passed the filter).
   The conclusion does not follow without an independent argument for the
   rubric's validity.

2. **What:** Range restriction / selection effect is uncontrolled — every
   data point comes from candidates who already cleared the bar.
   **Where:** Paragraph two, the "twenty-three engineers we hired" cohort.
   **Why it matters:** Without scores from rejected candidates who were
   nonetheless hired elsewhere and tracked, the data cannot distinguish "the
   test predicts performance" from "the test predicts being hired by us".

3. **What:** The recruiter's "informal tracking" of candidates who decline
   the take-home is offered as evidence without any evidential basis being
   shown.
   **Where:** Paragraph four ("We do not have hard numbers on this, but the
   recruiter has been tracking it informally").
   **Why it matters:** The author concedes the absence of data in the same
   sentence in which they rely on the claim. This is the weakest empirical
   move in the piece and props up the "screens out candidates who would
   have struggled" leg of the conclusion.

4. **What:** The counter-argument against pairing (observation bias) is
   asserted without support and is structurally identical in form to the
   equity argument the author dismisses against the take-home.
   **Where:** Paragraph three ("candidates who interview frequently are
   simply better at performing under observation").
   **Why it matters:** If the author is willing to dismiss this kind of
   correlational claim when it argues against the take-home, they cannot
   consistently rely on it when it argues for the take-home.

5. **What:** Sample size and base-rate concerns are acknowledged ("not
   statistically airtight on a sample this small") but the author proceeds
   as though the acknowledgement neutralises the concern.
   **Where:** End of paragraph two.
   **Why it matters:** Qualifying a claim verbally does not strengthen it;
   the conclusion still rides on a twenty-three-person sample with no
   control group.
